items.mistake: Reporting training accuracy as the model's real-world performance
items.fix: Always report metrics on held-out validation or test data the model never trained on; treat training metrics as a fit diagnostic only.
items.why: Training metrics measure memorization on seen rows — they systematically overstate performance on unseen production data when overfitting occurs.
items.example: Wrong: '99% accurate' from clf.predict(X_train). Right: accuracy_score(y_test, clf.predict(X_test)) after fit(X_train, y_train) only.

items.mistake: Tuning hyperparameters using the final test set repeatedly
items.fix: Use validation or cross-validation for model selection; touch the test set once (or rarely) for an unbiased generalization estimate.
items.why: Each test-set peek leaks information into choices — the test set stops being unseen and generalization estimates become optimistic.
items.example: Try 20 depths using CV scores; pick best depth, retrain on train+val, then evaluate once on the locked test split.

items.mistake: Including validation or test rows in fit()
items.fix: Split data before any preprocessing that learns statistics; call fit() only on training indices; apply transforms and predict on held-out rows.
items.why: Rows used in fit() are no longer unseen — metrics on them measure fit, not generalization.
items.example: Wrong: StandardScaler().fit(full_X) then train_test_split. Right: split first, scaler.fit(X_train), scaler.transform(X_test).

items.mistake: Assuming low training loss guarantees deployment success
items.fix: Monitor validation/test error and generalization gap; investigate distribution shift when production inputs diverge from training.
items.why: Models can minimize training loss by memorizing noise or exploiting spurious correlations that do not transfer to new data.
items.example: Hospital model with 0.05 train loss but 0.40 val loss on future-year admissions — do not deploy without addressing the gap.
